sections.header: Verses 1–10 — "The Heavenly Dwelling and the Judgment Seat"

heb.paragraph: The noun skēnos (tent) in v. 1 describes the present mortal body as a temporary dwelling. The metaphor draws on Israel’s wilderness tabernacle—a portable, impermanent structure—and contrasts it with the ‘building from God, an eternal house in heaven’ (oikia ek theou). The tent/building contrast expresses the same continuity-and-discontinuity that Paul articulated with the seed/plant metaphor in 1 Cor 15:36–44. The present body is real but temporary; the resurrection body is permanent and divine in origin.

The phrase bēma tou Christou (‘judgment seat of Christ,’ v. 10) invokes the Roman bēma—the elevated platform from which magistrates rendered verdicts. The Corinthians knew the bēma intimately: the stone platform in the Corinthian agora where Gallio judged Paul (Acts 18:12–17) has been archaeologically identified. Christ’s judgment seat is the eschatological tribunal at which believers’ works are assessed. The judgment is not salvific (believers are already reconciled) but evaluative: ‘each of us may receive what is due us for the things done while in the body.’

refs.note: Paul’s extended treatment of the resurrection body in 1 Cor 15 provides the theological backdrop for the tent/building contrast here. Both passages affirm bodily continuity through radical transformation.
Paul’s desire to ‘depart and be with Christ, which is better by far’ parallels 2 Cor 5:8 and confirms that Paul expected conscious communion with Christ immediately after death.
Paul’s parallel reference to ‘the judgment seat of God’ (some MSS: ‘of Christ’) in Romans confirms that the eschatological judgment of believers’ works is a consistent element of Pauline theology.

notes.note: The tent/building metaphor describes the transition from the mortal body to the resurrection body. Paul’s ‘groaning’ (vv. 2, 4) is not a complaint about embodiment but a longing for the completion of redemption. He does not desire to be ‘unclothed’ (disembodied) but ‘clothed’ with the heavenly dwelling (v. 4). The Spirit as ‘deposit’ (arrabōn, v. 5; cf. 1:22) guarantees the future transformation.

Paul’s confidence (v. 6, 8) is grounded in the Spirit’s guarantee. The preference to be ‘away from the body and at home with the Lord’ (v. 8) affirms a conscious intermediate state: death brings the believer into Christ’s presence even before the resurrection. The judgment-seat declaration (v. 10) provides eschatological accountability: every believer will give account for conduct in the body.

Calvin reads the tent/building contrast as a strong affirmation of the intermediate state: the soul, upon death, enters the Lord’s presence while awaiting the resurrection of the body. Calvin insists, against the Anabaptists of his day, that the soul does not ‘sleep’ between death and resurrection but enjoys conscious fellowship with Christ. The judgment-seat passage (v. 10) teaches that believers, though justified by faith, will have their works evaluated—not for salvation but for reward.

The NET note discusses the identity of the ‘building from God’: is it the individual resurrection body, the corporate body of Christ, or a heavenly temple? The NET favours the individual resurrection body, paralleling the tent (= present body) with the building (= resurrection body).

The NET note on the bēma (judgment seat) explains its Graeco-Roman background: the raised platform from which officials rendered judgments. The Corinthian bēma has been excavated in the Roman forum. Christ’s bēma is the eschatological equivalent.

Fee argues that this passage develops the already/not-yet tension introduced in 1 Cor 15: believers live between the first instalment (the Spirit as arrabōn) and the final consummation (the resurrection body). Fee reads Paul’s preference for being ‘with the Lord’ (v. 8) as evidence that Paul’s eschatology evolved between 1 Thessalonians (where the focus is on the parousia) and 2 Corinthians (where the focus shifts to include the individual’s experience after death).

Harris provides the definitive scholarly treatment of this passage, having devoted an entire monograph (Raised Immortal) to its exegesis. He argues that the ‘building from God’ (v. 1) is the individual resurrection body, not a heavenly dwelling entered at death. The ‘groaning’ (vv. 2, 4) expresses the tension of living in the ‘already’ of redemption while awaiting the ‘not yet’ of bodily transformation. Harris reads ‘away from the body’ (v. 8) as implying a conscious but ‘unclothed’ intermediate state—less than ideal but vastly preferable to being away from the Lord. The bēma judgment (v. 10) is the theological capstone: eschatological accountability motivates present faithfulness.

hist.context: This passage is one of the most theologically dense in the Pauline corpus, addressing the intermediate state (what happens between death and resurrection), the resurrection body, and the final judgment. Paul’s preference to be ‘away from the body and at home with the Lord’ (v. 8) has been central to discussions of Paul’s eschatology: does he envision a conscious intermediate state between death and resurrection? The majority view is yes: ‘at home with the Lord’ implies conscious fellowship with Christ after death, prior to the final resurrection. The judgment-seat declaration (v. 10) provides ethical motivation: believers will give account for their embodied conduct.

heb.gloss: to compel, to constrain, to press together
heb.paragraph: The verb synechō (to compel, to constrain) in v. 14 describes the force of Christ’s love on Paul’s ministry. The term denotes being hemmed in, pressed from both sides, with no escape. Paul is not driven by duty or ambition but by the irresistible pressure of Christ’s sacrificial love. The love that ‘compels’ is not Paul’s love for Christ but Christ’s love for Paul—the objective reality of the atonement that leaves no option but total surrender.

refs.note: Paul’s declaration that believers have ‘died with Christ’ (Rom 6) provides the fuller theological framework for the compressed statement ‘one died for all, and therefore all died’ (2 Cor 5:14).
‘I have been crucified with Christ and I no longer live, but Christ lives in me’ expresses the same participatory logic: Christ’s death is the believer’s death.

notes.note: Paul’s self-defence combines fear and love: the fear of the Lord (eschatological accountability) drives his persuasion of others (v. 11), while Christ’s love (v. 14) provides the inner compulsion. The charge of being ‘out of our mind’ (v. 13) may refer to ecstatic experiences (cf. 12:1–4) or simply to the intensity of Paul’s passion for ministry.

The atonement logic is tight: one died for all (substitutionary representation); therefore all died (participatory union); therefore those who live should no longer live for themselves (ethical transformation). The three steps—substitution, participation, transformation—summarise Paul’s soteriology in two verses.

Calvin reads ‘Christ’s love compels us’ as the heart of authentic ministry: the minister is driven not by human ambition or external pressure but by the constraining force of Christ’s self-giving love. Calvin analyses the substitutionary logic of v. 14: Christ’s death is ‘for all’ in the sense of ‘in the place of all’—his representative death becomes their death, so that they might live no longer for themselves but for him.

The NET note discusses the debated meaning of hyper pantōn (‘for all’): substitutionary (‘in the place of all’), representative (‘on behalf of all’), or both. The NET favours a combination: Christ died as the representative substitute for all, so that his death counts as their death.

Fee reads vv. 14–15 as the most concentrated atonement theology in 2 Corinthians. Christ’s love is not a subjective emotion but the objective reality of the cross: the one who died for all has incorporated all into his death so that all might live for him. Fee notes that the ethical implication (‘no longer live for themselves’) is the key: the super-apostles live for themselves; Paul lives for Christ.

Harris provides meticulous grammatical analysis of the atonement logic, arguing that the aorist ‘died’ (apethanen) in v. 14b refers to believers’ participatory death with Christ, not to physical death. The hina (‘so that’) clause of v. 15 specifies the purpose of Christ’s death: the transformation of the living from self-centred to Christ-centred existence. Harris reads this as a compressed version of the more developed argument in Rom 6:1–14.

hist.context: Paul transitions from eschatological motivation (vv. 1–10) to christological motivation (vv. 11–15). The ‘fear of the Lord’ (v. 11) connects to the judgment seat of v. 10: awareness of eschatological accountability drives Paul’s persuasive ministry. The charge that Paul is ‘out of his mind’ (v. 13) reflects Corinthian criticism of his ecstatic experiences or intense behaviour. Paul’s response pivots on v. 14: Christ’s love is the controlling force. The atonement summary (v. 14b–15) is dense: ‘one died for all, and therefore all died’—Christ’s representative death is appropriated by all who are ‘in him,’ resulting in a new orientation of life (v. 15).

heb.paragraph: The noun katallagē (reconciliation) and its cognate verb katallassein appear five times in vv. 18–20, making this the most concentrated reconciliation passage in the NT. Reconciliation presupposes a prior state of enmity between God and humanity. It is emphatically God’s initiative: God reconciled the world to himself (v. 19), not humanity reconciling itself to God. The ambassadorial metaphor (v. 20) positions Paul as God’s diplomat, carrying the reconciling message from the divine court to the estranged world.

The phrase kainē ktisis (‘new creation,’ v. 17) is one of Paul’s most theologically loaded expressions. It describes not merely personal renovation but cosmic re-creation: ‘The old has gone, the new is here!’ The term echoes Isaiah’s new-creation prophecy (Isa 65:17; 66:22) and signals that the eschaton has broken into the present. For Paul, being ‘in Christ’ is not a private spiritual experience but participation in God’s new-creation project.

refs.note: Isaiah’s prophecy of ‘new heavens and a new earth’ provides the OT background for Paul’s ‘new creation’ language. Paul applies the cosmic promise to the individual believer’s transformation in Christ.

Paul’s reconciliation language in Romans parallels 2 Cor 5: ‘While we were God’s enemies, we were reconciled to him through the death of his Son.’ Both passages emphasise divine initiative and the cross as the means of reconciliation.
Paul’s declaration that ‘neither circumcision nor uncircumcision means anything; what counts is the new creation’ confirms that ‘new creation’ is a fundamental Pauline category.

notes.note: Paul no longer evaluates anyone ‘from a worldly point of view’ (kata sarka, v. 16). Even Christ is no longer known ‘after the flesh’—the earthly, pre-resurrection Jesus is now the exalted Lord. Verse 17 announces the new-creation reality: anyone ‘in Christ’ is a new creation—the old order of sin, death, and worldly evaluation has passed away.

The reconciliation theology is entirely theocentric: God reconciles, God gives, God commits, God appeals. Paul is merely the ambassador—the diplomat who carries the message of peace from the divine court. The climactic v. 21 is the most concentrated substitutionary atonement statement in the NT: God made Christ, who knew no sin, ‘to be sin’ (not merely a sin-offering but the embodiment of sin’s curse) so that ‘in him we might become the righteousness of God.’ The great exchange: our sin imputed to Christ, his righteousness imputed to us.

Calvin regards v. 21 as the definitive biblical statement of imputed righteousness. Christ’s sinlessness is the presupposition: only the sinless one can bear sin vicariously. The ‘righteousness of God’ that believers ‘become’ is not their own moral achievement but an alien righteousness received by faith. Calvin reads the entire reconciliation passage as proof that salvation is entirely God’s work from start to finish: God initiates reconciliation, provides the means (Christ’s atoning death), and commissions the message (the ministry of reconciliation).

The NET note discusses whether kainē ktisis describes the individual believer (‘a new creation’) or the new eschatological order (‘there is a new creation’). The NET translators opt for ‘the new creation has come,’ interpreting the phrase as describing the new eschatological reality that has dawned in Christ.

The NET note on ‘made him to be sin’ (hamartian epoiēsen) discusses three main interpretations: (1) Christ was made a sin-offering; (2) Christ was made to bear sin’s penalty; (3) Christ was identified with sin itself in a representative capacity. The NET favours option 3, noting the parallel with ‘become the righteousness of God’: as believers do not become intrinsically righteous, so Christ did not become intrinsically sinful.

Fee reads the new-creation declaration as the hermeneutical key to the entire passage: the cross has inaugurated a new order of reality in which the old categories (worldly evaluation, self-centred living, enmity with God) have been replaced by reconciliation, new creation, and Christ-centred existence. Fee argues that v. 21 is not merely a statement about individual salvation but about God’s cosmic reconciliation project: in Christ, God is putting the whole world right.

Harris provides the most extensive treatment of v. 21 in modern commentary. He argues that ‘made him to be sin’ involves a double metonymy: Christ was treated as if he were sin (bearing its consequences) so that believers might be treated as if they were righteous (receiving God’s approval). Harris insists that the exchange is not merely forensic (a legal declaration) but transformative (believers actually participate in God’s righteousness through union with Christ). The reconciliation theology of vv. 18–20 is, for Harris, the climax of Paul’s apostolic apologia: Paul’s ministry is nothing less than God’s own reconciling appeal channelled through a human ambassador.

hist.context: This passage contains some of the most quoted and theologically consequential verses in the Pauline corpus. The ‘new creation’ declaration (v. 17) is the hinge: the old way of evaluating people (‘from a worldly point of view,’ kata sarka) has been replaced by a new-creation perspective. The reconciliation theology (vv. 18–20) is remarkable for its emphasis on divine initiative: God reconciles, God gives the ministry, God commits the message, God makes the appeal through the apostles. The final verse (v. 21) is perhaps the most profound substitutionary atonement statement in Paul: God made the sinless Christ ‘to be sin’ so that believers might ‘become the righteousness of God.’ The double exchange—sin imputed to Christ, righteousness imputed to believers—is the theological foundation of the Reformation doctrine of justification.

rows.label: vv.1–5
rows.text: Tent and building: mortal body, resurrection body, Spirit as guarantee

rows.label: vv.6–10
rows.text: Confidence, preference for the Lord’s presence, judgment seat

rows.label: vv.11–15
rows.text: Christ’s love compels: substitutionary death, participatory living

rows.label: vv.16–17
rows.text: New creation: the old has gone, the new is here

rows.label: vv.18–21
rows.text: Ministry of reconciliation: ambassadors, the great exchange of v. 21

lit.note: Chapter 5 is the theological summit of 2 Corinthians, moving from eschatology (intermediate state, judgment) through christology (Christ’s compelling love) to soteriology (new creation, reconciliation, the great exchange of v. 21).

themes.note: Mission peaks as Paul describes the ambassadorial ministry of reconciliation. Multiple themes score highly in this densely theological chapter.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 8) has been central to discussions of Paul’s eschatology: does he envision a conscious intermediate state between death and resurrection? The majority view is yes: ‘at home with the Lord’ implies conscious fellowship with Christ after death…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Ministry of Reconciliation

translations.text: Now the one who has fashioned us for this very purpose is God, who has given us the Spirit as a deposit, guaranteeing what is to come.
He who has prepared us for this very thing is God, who has given us the Spirit as a guarantee.
So we make it our goal to please him, whether we are at home in the body or away from it.
So whether we are at home or away, we make it our aim to please him.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Ministry of Reconciliation within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

discourse.thesis: We are ambassadors for Christ, carrying the ministry of reconciliation — God made him who knew no sin to be sin for us.

nodes.text: If our earthly tent is destroyed, we have a building from God, eternal in the heavens. We groan, longing to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling.
We walk by faith, not sight. We would rather be away from the body and at home with the Lord. We make it our aim to please him, for we must all appear before Christ's judgment seat.
We persuade others knowing the fear of the Lord. Christ's love controls us — if one died for all, then all died, so that the living might live for him.
If anyone is in Christ — new creation! God reconciled us through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation. We are ambassadors. God made him who knew no sin to be sin for us.
